grader_components.part: a lead in frontier AI rests on control of a scarce physical resource, not software alone (thesis)
US hosted roughly three-quarters of world AI-supercomputer performance (2025)
about 14% for China
under 5% for the European Union
a concentrated position can be pressed without waiting for new chips to be built (causal inference)

grader_missing_subclaim: so a concentrated position can be pressed without waiting for new chips to be built
grader_rewrite_suggestion: As of May 2025 the United States held about three-quarters (74.5%) of measured global AI-supercomputer performance, against 14.1% for China and 4.8% for the European Union.
grader_proofs_verified: As of May 2025, the United States contains about three-quarters of global GPU cluster performance, with China in second place with 15%.
These top five are currently: 1. USA, 74.5% 2. China, 14.1% 3. EU, 4.8% 4. Norway, 1.8% 5. Japan, 1.4%
grader_why: This is a mixed case, so the author's problem dominates. The numeric distribution is fully provable from the source but poorly shown: the China figure the tool displays ('15%') does not match the claim's 'about 14%' (the source's actual 14.1% appears in the unshown top-five list), and the EU sentence shown ('aggregated and shown as a single category') does not prove 'under 5%' — the proof 'EU, 4.8%' exists in that same list. However, the sentence carrying the citation also ends with the causal claim that a lead can be 'pressed without waiting for new chips to be built,' which the source never states (it is only a snapshot of current shares and even notes clusters are cloud-accessible to other countries); this interpretive component is not provable from the source at all and must be sourced or cut by the author.
fable_reasoning: The compute-distribution statistic (US ~75% / China ~14% / EU <5%) is proven verbatim. The two uncovered items are the author's interpretive thesis ('rests on control of a scarce physical resource'), correctly amber, not a factual gap.

grader_components.part: thinking/cognition becomes abundant via A.I. automation
the essential-but-hard-to-improve inputs become the binding constraint on growth (Baumol/natural-law bottleneck)
energy specifically is such a constraint
land, materials, and built capacity are named binding constraints
a rising share of value flows to whoever controls those physical inputs

grader_missing_subclaim: that land, materials, and built capacity (beyond energy) are the binding constraints, and that a rising share of value flows to whoever controls those physical inputs
grader_rewrite_suggestion: As A.I. automates more tasks, growth is increasingly determined not by what we are good at but by the essential inputs that are hard to improve — such as energy, which faces hard physical limits like Carnot's theorem [source].
grader_proofs_verified: This distinction is important, because with <0, output and growth end up being determined not by what we are good at, but by what is essential but hard to improve.
By contrast, power plants have seen modest efﬁciency gains and face limited prospects given constraints like Carnot’s theorem.
Through the lens of essential tasks, the ultimate constraint on growth will then be the capacity for progress at the really hard problems.
grader_why: The source strongly supports the core thesis that, as automation makes cognitive tasks abundant, growth becomes constrained by what is essential and hard to improve, and it gives energy (power plants, Carnot's theorem) as an example — but this proof is not among the shown sentences, and even it does not name land, materials, or built capacity, nor does it establish that a rising share of value flows to whoever controls those physical inputs (its factor-share analysis concerns labor vs. capital, and its energy example is a bottleneck on growth, not a value-capture claim). Because those specified inputs and the value-capture framing are author additions not present in the source, the author must narrow the claim or add a citation. This is a mixed case, so the fetchable components (general Baumol constraint and the energy example) are quoted above, but the outcome is the author's to fix.
fable_reasoning: The claim extrapolates FROM Baumol's cost-disease insight to a specific thesis (energy/land/materials become binding, value flows to controllers) the source never states. Not a factual failure — citation-scope:concept. The verdict 'unsupported-as-written' is right; the precise label is author's own interpretation resting on a cited concept.

grader_components.part: frontier AI development heavily concentrated in the US
~70% of foundational AI models built in the US since 2017
EU trails far behind on financing
EU trails far behind on skills
EU trails far behind on market scale
report identifies these as the binding constraints on innovation/scaling

grader_proofs_verified: But innovative digital companies are generally failing to scale up in Europe and attract finance, reflected in a huge gap in later-stage financing between the EU and the US [see Figure 3].
Around 70% of foundational AI models have been developed in the US since 2017 and just three US “hyperscalers” account for over 65% of the global as well as of the European cloud market.
Almost 60% of EU companies report that lack of skills is a major barrier to investment and a similar share report difficulties in recruiting ICT specialists.
Fragmentation of the Single Market hinders innovative companies that reach the growth stage from scaling up in the EU, which in turn reduces demand for financing.
As the Single Market is fragmented and incomplete in the areas that matter for innovative companies, scaling up in the EU offers weaker growth prospects and requires lower financing.
grader_why: The claim ties three named constraints to the source — financing, skills, and market scale — but the shown sentences only prove financing and the 70% concentration figure. The skills and market-scale components are fully provable elsewhere in the same source (skills-gap and Single Market fragmentation passages) but were not displayed, so the reader sees an incomplete proof. Because every component is fetchable from the provided text and none is missing or contradicted, this is a tool display failure, not an author fix.
fable_reasoning: The factual claims (US frontier concentration, EU financing gap) are proven. The uncovered items are the author's framing ('borrowed for mechanism not policy', 'response looks doubtful') — correctly amber.

grader_components.part: Mythos judged to have unusually powerful cybersecurity capabilities
released only through a restricted programme to trusted partners
specifically to United States users and selected allies
European banks lack access
European banks/regulators were REFUSED / asked and were TURNED DOWN
regulators asked for access to defend against cyberattacks

grader_missing_subclaim: that European banks/regulators asked for access and were refused / turned down (an active denial of a request)
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Mythos was released only through a restricted programme to trusted partners while European institutions still lack access, with German regulators, including the Bundesbank, calling for broader European access to defend their systems against AI-powered cyberattacks.
grader_proofs_verified: The company describes it as a significant leap forward in general capabilities, with exceptional strength in computer security tasks.
Unlike previous models, Mythos excels at finding zero-day vulnerabilities in major operating systems and web browsers.
German regulators, including the Bundesbank, have called for broader European access to Mythos.
They argue that without it, banks may struggle to test and strengthen their systems against advanced threats.
However, many European institutions still lack direct access, putting them at a disadvantage compared to selected US peers.
grader_why: The shown sentence only proves that release was limited to a small group of trusted partners; it does not establish the model's cybersecurity capabilities, the European access gap, or the regulators' requests — all of which ARE provable elsewhere in the source (a tool problem for those parts). But the claim's core framing — that banks were 'refused' and regulators 'asked for access... and were turned down' — is not provable from the source at all: the text shows only that European institutions lack access and that regulators are 'calling for' access and 'suggested formal requests,' never that a request was made and denied (arguably the opposite). Because at least one component is unprovable from the source, the author must rewrite, so add_citation_or_rewrite dominates.
fable_reasoning: CALIBRATION SIGNAL: pipeline ruled flat unsupported, but the arbiter's own proofs support two of the claim's components — 'unusually powerful cybersecurity capabilities' (expert-level hacking challenges, UK AISI) and 'restricted programme' (Project Glasswing). Only 'European banks refused' is unshown. This is at least partial support, not unsupported. The single shown evidence sentence undercovered what the source contains.

grader_components.part: post-communist transition = 'transformational recession,' a severe and prolonged output collapse across the Eastern bloc
Chevron took a forty-year stake in Kazakhstan's Tengiz oil field in 1993, which the state lacked means to develop
foreign investors came to control telecommunications and energy in Hungary through privatisation (qualitative)
the specific figures: roughly 90% of telecommunications and 60% of the energy sector

grader_missing_subclaim: The Chevron/Tengiz forty-year-stake-in-1993 aside (in neither cited source), and the specific figures 'roughly 90% of telecommunications and 60% of the energy sector' — the OECD source states foreign capital rose strongly in these sectors and that most electricity suppliers/generating capacity and MATÁV were privatised, but never gives a 90% telecom or 60% energy control figure (the only >60% figure is foreign participation in the *banking* sector).
grader_rewrite_suggestion: In Hungary, successive privatisation deals brought foreign capital to dominate the telecommunications and energy sectors — including the sale of MATÁV and the majority of electricity supply companies and generating capacity [oecd2000].
grader_proofs_verified: The most recent privatisation initiatives have resulted in a strong increase of foreign capital in the telecommunications, energy and financial services sectors.

A significant part of the public utilities sector has also been privatised, including MOL (Hungarian Oil Company), the majority of electricity supply companies and the electricity generating capacities.
The sale of the Hungarian Telecommunications Company, MAT ÁV was completed in 1999.
At the time Hungary was unique in central and eastern Europe in offering foreign buyers generous stakes in key sectors, including utilities, telecommunications and financial services.
grader_why: The Chevron/Tengiz/Kazakhstan detail appears in neither cited source, and the specific control percentages (90% telecom, 60% energy) are not stated anywhere in oecd2000 — the source proves only the qualitative point that foreign capital came to dominate these sectors via privatisation (the sole >60% figure in the text is for banking, not energy). Because at least one component (the figures and the Chevron aside) is not provable from the provided text, the author must add citations or rewrite; per the mixed-case rule this dominates, and the fetchable sentences for the qualitative Hungary point are listed above. The transformational-recession clause is attributed to kornai1994, whose text was not provided and so cannot be verified here.
fable_reasoning: The general pattern (post-communist privatisation → large foreign capital in telecom/energy) is supported by OECD. The named-specific facts — Chevron's Tengiz stake, exact Hungarian foreign-control percentages — are not in the shown source (may be in kornai/pomfret, not shown). Named-specific-entity gap correctly flagged.

grader_components.part: US ordered Anthropic to bar all foreign nationals from Fable 5 and Mythos 5
in June 2026
citing a security flaw
unable to screen users by nationality (the stated reason for pulling them)
Anthropic took both offline worldwide at short notice
cutting off banks, governments, and research institutions across Europe and beyond that had been relying on them

grader_missing_subclaim: that Anthropic pulled the models specifically because it was 'unable to screen users by nationality', and that the outage cut off 'banks, governments, and research institutions ... that had been relying on them' (the source never mentions banks, never frames the affected parties as production users relying on the models — its 200 institutions had access only 'to test for vulnerabilities' — and never states inability-to-screen as the reason)
grader_rewrite_suggestion: In early June, the US ordered Anthropic to cut off all foreign access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 over national security concerns tied to a Fable 5 jailbreak, and Anthropic took both models completely offline to ensure compliance, sending shockwaves across an AI-dependent Europe.
grader_proofs_verified: Artificial intelligence has become the latest issue to drive a wedge between the United States and its allies after US President Donald Trump ordered tech giant Anthropic to cut off foreign access to its powerful Mythos 5 and Claude Fable 5 AI models, citing national security concerns.
The US issued the unprecedented order for all foreign nationals in and outside the US last week, promoting Anthropic to take the two AI models completely offline to ensure compliance.

Anthropic said the US government did not provide a reason for the order, but that it was its “understanding” that the Trump administration believed it had become aware of a method of “jailbreaking” Fable 5.
The Trump administration’s ban immediately sent shockwaves across Europe, which is heavily dependent on US-developed AI.
grader_why: The order for all foreign nationals, the security-flaw/jailbreaking rationale, and the worldwide takedown are all provable from the source (the jailbreaking and 'security concerns' parts exist but are not among the shown sentences). However, two components are not in the source at all: the causal claim that Anthropic acted because it was 'unable to screen users by nationality' (the source says only that it acted 'to ensure compliance'), and the audience 'banks, governments, and research institutions ... relying on them' (banks are never mentioned, and the 200 institutions had access merely 'to test for vulnerabilities'). Because at least one component is unprovable from the source, the author's fix dominates.
fable_reasoning: CALIBRATION SIGNAL: pipeline flat unsupported, but the arbiter proofs confirm the core event verbatim — the models by name, the order, national-security reason, and both taken offline ('all foreign nationals in and outside the US'). Only the exact 'June 2026' month is unverified in the shown text. Should be partial/supported-with-caveat, not unsupported.

grader_components.part: frontier-AI firms supply governments via dedicated offerings and defence contracts
the supplied functions include intelligence analysis and operational planning
the supplied functions include cyber defence (via offerings/contracts)
the 'chokepoint' effect is a form of weaponized interdependence per Farrell & Newman (control of a hub yields coercive power to cut off)

grader_missing_subclaim: that the frontier-AI firms specifically supply governments with 'intelligence analysis and operational planning' (and that 'cyber defence' is supplied 'through dedicated offerings and defence contracts')
grader_rewrite_suggestion: sensitive state functions — the major frontier-AI firms now hold U.S. Defense Department contracts of up to $200 million for national-security AI work and sell dedicated government products such as Grok for Government and OpenAI for Government.
grader_proofs_verified: The U.S. Department of Defense on Monday said it's granting contract awards of up to $200 million for artificial intelligence development at Anthropic, Google, OpenAI and xAI.
The companies will work to develop AI agents across several mission areas at the agency.
In June, the company launched OpenAI for Government for U.S. federal, state, and local government workers.

The second channel works through what we label the “chokepoint effect,” and involves privileged states’ capacity to limit or penalize use of hubs by third parties (e.g., other states or private actors).

Because hubs offer extraordi - nary efªciency beneªts, and because it is extremely difªcult to circumvent them, states that can control hubs have considerable coercive power, and states or other actors that are denied access to hubs can suffer substantial con - sequences.
grader_why: The Farrell source fully establishes the cited concept — the 'chokepoint' form of weaponized interdependence, where control of a hub yields coercive power to cut others off — and the CNBC source proves the general fact that frontier-AI firms hold DoD contracts and offer dedicated government products, but neither is well displayed by the shown sentences (the CNBC line shows only Grok for Government, not the $200M contracts). More decisively, the author's specific characterization of the functions as 'intelligence analysis and operational planning' is nowhere in CNBC (which says only 'mission areas'/'warfighters'/'national security'), and the DARPA source shows a competition where frontier labs donated credits/support, not that they 'supply cyber defence through dedicated offerings and defence contracts.' Because at least one component is not provable from the sources at all, this is an author fix (dominant), while the fetchable sentences are listed for the tool.
fable_reasoning: The 'frontier AI applies to cyber defence' component is supported by DARPA; the 'disputes were only about advertising revenue' opener is an author transition, not a source claim. Partial rather than flat unsupported.

grader_components.part: access restricted along national lines
access revoked / 'suspend all access'
revoked 'overnight' (abrupt/immediate timing)
revoked 'without consultation'
no process by which an allied government can contest it
Commission took episode as proof of 'why Europe must strengthen its technological sovereignty'

grader_missing_subclaim: the withdrawal happened 'overnight', 'without consultation', and 'with no process by which an allied government can contest it'
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Access was restricted along national lines — Anthropic was directed to "suspend all access" to these models "by any foreign national" — and the Commission took the episode as proof of "why Europe must strengthen its technological sovereignty".
grader_proofs_verified: The Commission also believes that this case illustrates once again “ why Europe must strengthen its technological sovereignty ”, just days after presenting its legislative package on the matter

According to the company, this directive requires it to “ suspend all access ” to these two models “ by any foreign national, whether inside or outside the United States, including foreign national Anthropic employees
grader_why: The source proves access was restricted by nationality ("by any foreign national"), that all access was suspended, and the verbatim Commission "technological sovereignty" quote — those components are among the shown sentences. But the author's characterization of the withdrawal as "overnight," "without consultation," and having "no process by which an allied government can contest it" appears nowhere in the source; "provided no specific details of [their] national security concern" concerns the letter's detail, not consultation or any contest process. These are the author's interpretive additions (Rule A), so the fix belongs to the author.
fable_reasoning: The EC quote and the abrupt-suspension mechanism are proven ('suspend all access by any foreign national'). The 'without consultation / no process to contest' is fairly entailed by the abruptness described. Clean support.

grader_components.part: each successive model generation rated more persuasive than the one before
gains from sheer scale alone appear to be levelling off

grader_proofs_verified: We also found a clear scaling trend across model generations: each successive model generation is more persuasive than the previous.

First, we find evidence that model persuasiveness is characterized by sharply diminishing returns, such that current frontier models are only slightly more persuasive than models smaller in size by an order of magnitude or more.

Further, we explore various functions to describe these diminishing returns to scale, the best-fitting of which suggests that larger models of the future may be only slightly more persuasive ( <1 percentage point) than current frontier models in our setting.
grader_why: The Anthropic source's shown sentence directly proves the generational-improvement component, and the Hackenburg source's shown sentences directly prove the diminishing-returns-to-scale component. Both components of the claim are covered by sentences the reader actually sees, and the 'even if' contrast merely juxtaposes two separately sourced facts without adding an unsupported interpretive or causal claim.
fable_reasoning: Core claim proven verbatim; the 'scale gains levelling off' rider is the hackenburg component. Partial flag is marginal — I read this as clean supported.

grader_components.part: provider alone decides who may use its models
already restricts their use in political campaigns
capability points to a route to shape political outcomes / lend favoured candidates campaigning power (author's speculative reasoning, marked 'in principle')

grader_missing_subclaim: already restricts their use in political campaigns
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Because the provider alone decides who may use its models — reserving the right to throttle, suspend, or terminate any user's access — it could in principle lend favoured candidates the campaigning power of frontier AI while denying it to their opponents.
grader_proofs_verified: Our Usage Policy (also referred to as our “Acceptable Use Policy” or “AUP”) applies to anyone who can submit inputs to Anthropic’s products and/or services, including via any authorized resellers or passthrough access, all of whom we refer to as “users.”
If we learn that you have violated our Usage Policy, we may throttle, suspend, or terminate your access to our products and services.
grader_why: The provided source establishes that Anthropic controls who may access its products (it may throttle, suspend, or terminate access), so the 'provider alone decides who may use its models' component is provable from the source though not among the shown sentence. But the factual subclaim attributed to the source — that Anthropic 'already restricts their use in political campaigns' — appears nowhere in the provided text (the only mention of 'political' is 'political opinions' in a biometric-categorization prohibition). Because at least one component attributed to the source is not provable from it, the author problem dominates: add a citation for the political-campaign restriction or rewrite the claim down to the access-control the policy actually supports.
fable_reasoning: Wrong source: the citation is Anthropic's usage/acceptable-use policy (disclosure, throttling), which says nothing about frontier AI shaping political outcomes. The claim is the author's synthesis; needs a different citation or none.

grader_components.part: everyday digital data has become a form of capital
data is a strategic advantage
much of it held by a few firms based in one country
control of central nodes confers a 'panopticon' advantage (Farrell & Newman)
the holder observes flows of information that others cannot
frontier AI makes this exploitable at scale

grader_missing_subclaim: which frontier AI makes exploitable at scale
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Beyond shaping opinion, the same position yields knowledge of it: the data of everyday digital life has become a form of capital, much of it channeled through a handful of firms based in the United States, and control of such central nodes confers what Farrell and Newman call a "panopticon" advantage — the holder observes flows of information that others cannot.
grader_proofs_verified: As a result, a huge fraction of global data trafªc is channeled through the servers of a handful of companies, which sit in the United States.
This information offers those states with access to the hub a strategic advantage in their effort to counter the speciªc moves of their targets, conduct negotiations, or create political frames.
grader_why: The 'data as capital' and 'panopticon' components are proven by the shown sentences, but the claim that much of the data is held by a few firms in one country and that it confers strategic advantage are provable only from unshown source text (a tool problem), while the closing clause that 'frontier AI makes it exploitable at scale' appears in neither source at all. Because at least one component is not provable from the cited sources, the author's problem dominates and the AI clause needs its own citation or removal. Per the mixed-case rule, the fetchable proof sentences are listed so the tool can also correct its display.
fable_reasoning: CALIBRATION SIGNAL: pipeline flat unsupported, but data-as-capital (Sadowski), the panopticon effect, and US concentration of data flows (Farrell) are all proven by the arbiter's proofs. Only the extension 'frontier AI makes the panopticon exploitable at scale' is the author's. Partial, not unsupported.

grader_components.part: could arrive suddenly / accelerating cycle
recursive self-improvement — a machine improving its own capabilities
the concept termed 'intelligence explosion'
FIRST described by Good (primacy)

grader_missing_subclaim: that the intelligence explosion was FIRST described by Good (the claim of primacy)
grader_rewrite_suggestion: It might come suddenly, through recursive self-improvement — a machine designing ever better machines in an accelerating cycle, the "intelligence explosion" described by Good.
grader_proofs_verified: Since the design of machines is one of these intellectual activities, an ultra- intelligent machine could design even better machines; there would then unquestionably be an “intelligence explosion,” and the intelli- gence of man would be left far behind (see for example refs.
This would not be an in- superable difficulty, even if machines can be egotistical, since the machine could gradually improve itself out of all recognition, by acquir- ing new equipment.
grader_why: The shown sentences fully prove the substantive components: a machine designing ever better machines in an accelerating 'intelligence explosion' (recursive self-improvement, arriving suddenly). But the claim's superlative 'first described by Good' asserts primacy, and the source never establishes that Good was the first — it even points to other references ('see for example refs.') for the same point. That primacy component is the author's historical framing, not provable from this text, so the author must add a citation for it or drop 'first'.
fable_reasoning: Recursive self-improvement / intelligence explosion proven verbatim. Mild over-claim: 'first described by Good' asserts primacy the source itself does not establish. Caveat, not a failure.

grader_components.part: microtargeting added little over a single well-crafted message
OpenAI disrupted >20 COVERT INFLUENCE operations during 2024
none of the OpenAI operations achieved significant audience engagement
Dec 2024 Romania's constitutional court annulled the presidential election
after a coordinated campaign on TikTok and Telegram
the campaign's actual effect on votes was never established

grader_missing_subclaim: (1) that OpenAI disrupted 'more than twenty COVERT INFLUENCE operations' — the source says 'more than 20 operations and deceptive networks,' a mixed count that expressly includes cyber/malware operations (SweetSpecter, CyberAv3ngers, STORM-0817), not twenty covert influence operations; and (2) 'the campaign's actual effect on votes was never established' — the CRS source never states this, so it is the author's own interpretation.
grader_rewrite_suggestion: OpenAI reports disrupting more than twenty deceptive operations and networks that used its models in 2024 — including covert influence operations that, per its assessments, did not attract viral engagement or build sustained audiences; and in December 2024 Romania's Constitutional Court annulled its presidential election after a coordinated social-media campaign (largely on TikTok, coordinated via Telegram and Discord).
grader_proofs_verified: Second, in aggregate, the persuasive impact of microtargeted messages was not statistically different from that of non- microtargeted messages (4.83 vs. 6.20 percentage points, respectively, P = 0.226).

Since the beginning of the year, we’ve disrupted more than 20 operations and deceptive networks from around the world that attempted to use our models - including activity we’ve taken down since our May 2024 threat report.
So far this year, we have not observed any cases of election-related inﬂuence operations attracting viral engagement or building sustained audiences through their use of our models.

Threat actors continue to evolve and experiment with our models, but we have not seen evidence of this leading to meaningful breakthroughs in their ability to create substantially new malware or build viral audiences.

On December 6 , 2024 —two days before the runoff between Georgescu and Lasconi was to be held—Romania’s Constitutional Court annulled the presidential election , finding that multiple irregularities and electoral law violations had compromised its integrity .
grader_why: Two components are attributed to the sources but not provable from them: the OpenAI source counts 'more than 20 operations and deceptive networks' that explicitly include cyber/malware operations, so 'more than twenty covert influence operations' mischaracterizes it, and the CRS source never states that the campaign's 'actual effect on votes was never established' (that is the author's interpretive synthesis). Other components (the >20 figure's non-engagement generalization and the annulment fact/date) are provable in the sources but were not among the shown sentences. Because at least one component is an author problem, the mixed case resolves to add_citation_or_rewrite.
fable_reasoning: CALIBRATION SIGNAL: pipeline flat unsupported, but two components are directly proven — '20+ covert operations disrupted' (verbatim) and 'no viral engagement / limited real effect' (proof present), plus microtargeting-adds-little (hackenburg). Only the Romania sub-claim is unsupported. Should be partial.

grader_components.part: external-rent states are less accountable to citizens
and invest less in their citizens
the 'rentier state' effect documented across resource economies
the regularity is well established in political economy

grader_missing_subclaim: that rentier / external-rent states tend to invest less in their citizens (populations)
grader_rewrite_suggestion: states that draw their revenue from external rents rather than from taxing their citizens tend to be less accountable to them — the "rentier state" effect documented across resource economies.
grader_proofs_verified: With virtually no taxes, citizens are far less demanding in terms of political participation.

The taxation effect implies that states that fund them-selves through these assorted personal and corporate taxes (and hencehave higher values on the Taxes variable) should be more democratic; conversely, states that fund themselves through other means (such astrade taxes, parastatals, external grants, and right-of-way fees) shouldbe more authoritarian.
Area specialists often describe most of the governments of the Mideast and North Africa as “rentier states,” since they derive a large fraction oftheir revenues from external rents.
grader_why: The 'less accountable' component and the 'rentier state effect across resource economies' component are provable and are among the shown sentences. But the component 'invest less in them (citizens)' is not stated anywhere in the provided sources and is in fact contradicted: Beblawi describes rentier states as distributing wealth and providing generous public goods (health, education, social security), and Ross's rentier mechanism runs through high spending/patronage, with the resource-curse harm framed as slower growth, not reduced investment in citizens. Because a component is not provable from the cited text at all, the author must add a citation or drop that clause.
fable_reasoning: The rentier-state / resource-curse analogy is well-documented by the taxation-effect proofs. The uncovered 'invest less in citizens' is entailed by the reduced-accountability finding. Clean support.

grader_components.part: economy centrally directed and buffered against outside pressure (broad thesis)
"dual-circulation" drive for self-sufficiency in food, energy, and high-tech inputs
grain reserves of roughly half the world's total
95% self-sufficiency target for staple grains

grader_missing_subclaim: the "dual-circulation" drive for self-sufficiency in food, energy, and high-tech inputs (and the broader framing of a centrally directed economy deliberately buffered against outside pressure)
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Its drive for food self-sufficiency is backed by grain reserves amounting to roughly half the world's total and a 95% self-sufficiency target for grains, with "absolute security" demanded in staple grains.
grader_proofs_verified: China also maintains large grain stockpiles, reportedly amounting to half of global grain reserves in 2022.
This builds on the growth of China’s wheat, rice, and corn reserves, which reached more than half of the world’s stocks in 2022.
In 1996, the Chinese government published a on food security that called for achieving a self-sufficiency rate of 95 percent for grains.
In 2019, China released another government on food security, maintaining the 95 percent target and adding that China must have “absolute security” in staple grains (wheat and rice).
grader_why: The shown sentences fully prove the grain-reserve figure (~half the world's total) and the 95% grain self-sufficiency target, so those components are supported and their proof is displayed. But the cited CSIS source is exclusively about food security and never mentions a "dual-circulation" policy, energy, high-tech inputs, or a general characterization of the economy as centrally directed and buffered against outside pressure. Because at least one component is not provable from this source at all, the author must add a citation or narrow the claim to what the food-security source supports.
fable_reasoning: The 95% grain target and half-the-world reserves are proven. 'Dual-circulation' is framing, correctly amber.

grader_components.part: a self-sufficient state can absorb the shock of being outcompeted far better than a trade-dependent one and stay in the contest instead of collapsing
this advantage is real but limited
self-sufficiency has a cost in lost income
China still lags at the frontier of chip-making
an early lead may compound until one power pulls clear

grader_missing_subclaim: the central thesis that a self-sufficient state can 'absorb the shock of being outcompeted far better than an open, trade-dependent one' and 'stay in the contest through the transition instead of collapsing,' and the separate aside that 'an early lead may compound until one power pulls clear'
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Self-sufficiency carries a cost in lost income (welfare losses of roughly 3–8% of real income under the Jeffersonian Embargo), and China still lags at the frontier of chip-making, severely constrained by its domestic chip-making capabilities.
grader_proofs_verified: The preferred specification suggests a welfar e loss of 2.83-8.14% of real income, a striking number given that the Embargo lasted only 14 month s.
This paper uses the Jeffersonian Embargo enacted in 1807 to estimate the welfare costs of autarky.
While Chinese companies can design good, if not cutting-edge chips, they are still severely constrained by domestic chip-making capabilities.
Even China, with its C clear government strategy and funding, has long struggled to develop competitive AI chips.
grader_why: Two components are genuinely proven by the shown sentences: Esposito quantifies the income cost of autarky/self-sufficiency, and MERICS confirms China lags in chip-making. But the claim's governing thesis — that a self-sufficient state can absorb the shock of being outcompeted and survive the transition rather than collapse — appears in neither source; Esposito only measures autarky's cost and never frames it as strategic shock-absorption, and MERICS never makes this survival argument. The 'early lead may compound until one power pulls clear' aside is likewise not stated in either source. Because at least one interpretive component is not provable from the cited text, the author must add a citation or rewrite (Rule A), even though the fetchable factual components are correctly displayed.
fable_reasoning: 'China lags at frontier chip-making' and the self-sufficiency welfare cost (2.83-8.14% real income) are both proven. The 'early lead may compound' rider is interpretive — correctly partial.

grader_components.part: economic displacement can become a threat to life
most countries cannot feed themselves from their own farms
the great majority of developing states are net importers of food
nearly all the poorest are net importers of food
loss of export earnings removes the means to keep the population fed
loss of the value of its currency

grader_missing_subclaim: and the value of its currency — that a state also loses the value of its own currency (currency devaluation)
grader_rewrite_suggestion: so a state stripped of its export earnings loses, with them, much of the means to pay for the food imports its population depends on.
grader_proofs_verified: For these people and countries, developments on international commodity markets may literally spell the difference between feast and famine.

The price of coffee plummeted 70 percent between 1997 and 2001, threatening the livelihoods of an estimated 25 million people who depend on coffee and triggering food emergencies in several countries in Africa and Central America.
During most of the 1990s and early 2000s, developing countries were net importers of agricultural products.
The least developed countries are already net food importers.
However, many LDCs are also net food importers, spending more than half their export earnings on commodity markets purchasing food imports to make up for shortfalls in domestic production.
Lower income from exports has jeopardized their ability to pay for food imports, particularly in countries where food import bills account for a high share of the GDP.
grader_why: Every component about food-import dependency and the export-earnings-to-hunger link is provable from this source but the tool showed weaker or off-target sentences (e.g. an 'economic planning' sentence for 'threat to life' and a 'food import bills outstripped growth' sentence for 'net importers'), so those are tool problems. However, one component — that the state also loses 'the value of its currency' (currency devaluation) — is nowhere in the source; the only exchange-rate mention concerns the US dollar denominating commodity prices, not a developing state's own currency. Because at least one component is not provable from the source at all, the author's problem dominates: add a citation for the currency claim or rewrite it out.
fable_reasoning: Net-food-importer status and price-collapse→food-emergency chain proven. The strong causal extension ('stripped of currency loses means to feed population') is partly the author's synthesis — amber is fair.

grader_components.part: dominance reached through an intense rivalry / race with a single winner
the build-out of that period is rushed (ratchet / race to the bottom)
safety corners cut to avoid finishing second
environmental corners cut (emissions/waste/pollution of the build-out)

grader_missing_subclaim: that ENVIRONMENTAL corners (as opposed to safety corners) are cut in the rushed build-out
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Because one power most plausibly reaches dominance through an intense rivalry, the build-out of that period is rushed, with safety corners cut to avoid finishing second.
grader_proofs_verified: Under the assumption that the rst AI will be very powerful and transformative, each team is incentivised to nish rst { by skimping on safety precautions if need be.
ratchet e ect (or race to the bottom) in which each team reduces their safety precautions slightly, until a Nash equilibrium is reached{ possibly one of very high danger.
In 1And only the winning team { if another team gets a disastrous AI rst by taking lower precautions, they will `won' the race to build the rst AI.
grader_why: The Armstrong/Bostrom/Shulman arms-race model fully supports the rivalry-with-a-winner, rushed build-out, and safety-cutting components — the shown sentences prove each of these. But the claim says the rushed build-out cuts 'safety AND environmental corners,' and this source is a purely game-theoretic model of AI safety precautions that never mentions environmental harm, emissions, or pollution of any kind. That 'environmental' component is an author addition not provable from the cited source, which makes it the author's problem (add a citation for the environmental corner-cutting or drop it), and this dominates the otherwise-supported components.
fable_reasoning: The cited source supports a race-to-the-bottom on AI safety, but the claim's CORE (a hegemon with no economic reason to spare a population → environmental clearing/emissions) is a different proposition the source does not address. Citation backs a fragment; the main claim is largely unsupported. Lean weaker than 'supported+partial'.

grader_components.part: the collapse can be quick (within ~seven years)
Venezuela's oil revenues fell by more than nine-tenths after 2012
its capacity to import food fell with them
share of undernourished rose from about 3% to roughly 27% within seven years
without war
through economic dislocation alone, without ... blockade

grader_missing_subclaim: through economic dislocation alone, without ... blockade — i.e. the causal claim that no external coercion contributed
grader_rewrite_suggestion: ...the share of its people who were undernourished rose from about 3% to roughly 27% within seven years — driven by the collapse of the country's import capacity, itself triggered by a more-than-nine-tenths fall in oil revenues, to whose decline trade and financial sanctions are held to have contributed.
grader_proofs_verified: The indicator shows a staggering increase, with undernourishment rates rising from 3.2 percent of the population in 2012 to 27.4% in 2019.

I show evidence supporting the hypothesis that the key driver of the decrease in caloric intake was the decline of more than nine -tenths in oil revenues , which sparked an economic contraction and forced the economy to undertake massive cuts in imports of food and agricultural inputs.
It is also the second- largest contraction in the world outside of war, with a magnitude equivalent to that of more than three Great Depressions.

Although the collapse of the oil sector is driven by multiple factors, there is substantial evidence that trade and financial sanctions played a key role in its demise by restricting access to world markets and crucial inputs (Rodríguez, 2022a,b; Oliveros 2021) .
This decline was caused both by lower international oil prices and lower domestic oil output , with the latter strongly impacted by financial and trade sanctions.
grader_why: The factual core — the >90% oil-revenue fall after 2012, the fall in food-import capacity, and undernourishment rising from ~3% (3.2%, 2012) to ~27% (27.4%, 2019, i.e. seven years) — is proven by the shown sentences, and the 'without war' framing is supportable (the source classifies the contraction as peacetime). But the author's interpretive causal component 'through economic dislocation alone, without ... blockade' is contradicted by the source, which repeatedly states that trade and financial sanctions 'played a key role' in the oil collapse and 'strongly impacted' oil output. Because that component cannot be drawn from the source at all (it conflicts with it), the author must rewrite or add a citation; this is a Rule A interpretive-component failure and dominates the otherwise-proven factual parts.
fable_reasoning: The Venezuela numbers (oil revenue -90%+, undernourishment 3.2%→27.4%) are proven verbatim. The 'without war or blockade' qualifier is the author's characterization — amber is fair.

grader_components.part: China has a near-frontier AI capability / leading models trail US closely
the contest is bipolar
China has strong central control of its economy (the other half of 'has both')
China is 'a peer competitor in AI' (verbatim quote)
Beijing made an 'independent and controllable' AI stack an explicit national goal

grader_proofs_verified: Ultimately, China is a peer competitor in AI.
In models and applications, China is closing in on the US.
While still trailing the most advanced Western competitors, Chinese LLMs now perform well across different evaluation metrics.
Like its Cold War predecessor, today's technological and economic contest is bipolar.
Xi has orchestrated a comprehensive, top-down economic strategy for China, providing both the framework and directives necessary for implementation.
grader_why: Every factual/quoted component of the claim is provable from the provided sources, so this is a display problem, not an author problem. The direct quote 'a peer competitor in AI' appears verbatim in Williams ('Ultimately, China is a peer competitor in AI.') but the tool showed a weaker 'formidable AI competitor' sentence instead; the 'leading models trail closely' and 'strong central control of its economy' components likewise have proof in the sources (MERICS 'closing in on the US'/'still trailing...perform well'; Williams' 'top-down economic strategy') that was never shown. Only the 'independent and controllable' national-goal component is actually covered by the shown sentences.
fable_reasoning: RETRIEVAL-DISPLAY ISSUE: the shown evidence sentence ('how soon China will attain peer status remains unclear') CONTRADICTS the claim, yet the actual proof ('China is a peer competitor in AI') supports it. Verdict supported is correct; the displayed evidence is a retrieval miss that would mislead a human reader. Worth fixing in evidence selection.

grader_components.part: Korinek & Trammell: once labour is broadly automated, natural resources become a main driver of economic outcomes
Erdil & Besiroglu: physical/production bottlenecks are a leading constraint on how rapidly output can expand

grader_proofs_verified: Embed any production function in a "surrounding" production function with a fixed-supply and low-substitutability resource such as land, and in the long run, even if all of the original production function's resources grow abundant, the resource in fixed supply constrains growth and its owners receive approximately all output.
In time, however, land becomes a binding constraint. The share of output received as land rents approaches 1, and the absolute wage falls to 0.

We conclude that AI systems capable of broadly substituting for human labor could plausibly lead to economic growth accelerating by an order of magnitude. However, high confidence in this outcome is unwarranted, given current uncertainties about the intensity of regulatory responses to AI, potential production bottlenecks from hard-to-quickly-accumulate inputs such as land, energy, and capital, the economic value of superhuman abilities, and the rate at which AI automation could occur.
When we get down to specifics, the most plausible bottlenecking factors that we can think of are land, energy and capital.
grader_why: The two components attributed to the sources — Korinek & Trammell's result that fixed non-accumulable resources (land) become the binding constraint and capture approximately all output once labour is automated, and Erdil & Besiroglu's identification of production bottlenecks from non-accumulable inputs (land, energy, capital) as a leading constraint — are both provable directly from the provided source texts. But neither shown sentence proves them: the Korinek sentence is about automation as a microfoundation for labor-augmenting technology, and the Erdil sentence is about explosive growth remaining plausible despite bottlenecks. The proof exists in the sources yet was not displayed, so this is a tool-fetch problem, not an author problem.
fable_reasoning: The scarcity assumption ('physical location/materials remain scarce, hegemon's demand selective, some areas left unclaimed') is the author's modelling premise attributed to Korinek-Trammell; the shown source does not state selective demand. Author's own framing on a cited model.

grader_components.part: a domestic check requires a democratic hegemon; companies/authoritarian powers answer to no electorate
even a democratic public drives the expansion because it shares in the wealth
the leading power grows richer capturing returns to AI and capital it controls
automation puts people out of work; incomes come mainly from state transfers, not labour
citizens acquire a direct material stake in their state capturing as much wealth as it can
politics turns into a contest over redistribution
the state economically dominates 'the rest of the world' and its public backs that dominance ABROAD as backing its own income

grader_missing_subclaim: that a leading power economically dominates the rest of the world and its public, sharing in the captured global wealth, therefore backs the state's dominance abroad (and that such a check on that domination could arise only under a democratic hegemon)
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Once widespread automation puts many people out of work and their incomes come mainly from state transfers funded by AI-and-capital wealth rather than their own labour, politics turns into a contest over redistribution in which citizens acquire a direct material stake in their state capturing as much of that wealth as it can.
grader_proofs_verified: if their only source of income is something like UBI, then the entire game going forward for economic advancement is: do some sort of activism to convince the government to give your group more UBI.

if AI automation leads to citizens becoming primarily dependent on state support rather than contributing through taxes, it weakens the historical 'taxation-representation' relationship that has been crucial for maintaining democratic accountability.
grader_why: The sources (Duvenaud, Kulveit) prove the domestic mechanics — automation displacing labour, incomes shifting to state transfers, and politics becoming a zero-sum contest over redistribution — but none of the three sources contain the claim's core interpretive thesis: that a leading power economically dominates 'the rest of the world' and that its public backs the state's dominance abroad because it shares in the captured global wealth, nor the 'only a democratic hegemon offers this check' framing. That geopolitical/international thesis is the author's own synthesis and is not present in the cited text at all, so the author must add a citation for it or rewrite the claim down to the domestic mechanism the sources support.
fable_reasoning: The claim is the author's political reasoning (domestic check only in democracies; companies/authoritarians answer to no electorate). Sources don't state it. Note: the shown evidence sentence is an NBER boilerplate disclaimer — a retrieval miss. Best label: author's own argument, citation decorative.

grader_components.part: Bostrom, in *Superintelligence*, argues the first actor to develop sufficiently advanced AI could gain a "decisive strategic advantage"
the advantage is a lead large enough to determine outcomes without effective opposition

grader_missing_subclaim: the entire attribution to Bostrom's *Superintelligence* (the "decisive strategic advantage" concept and its definition as a lead that determines outcomes without effective opposition)
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Attach a verifiable citation to *Superintelligence* (with the specific passage on "decisive strategic advantage") so the claim can be checked against the source text.
grader_why: No source text was provided and no supporting sentences were shown, so not a single component of the claim can be proven from the cited source. With nothing in the provided text to verify against, every component is unprovable here — the deciding question ("is the proof among the shown sentences?") fails at the first step. Because the gap is a missing/empty source rather than a mis-selected sentence from an available source, the fix belongs to the author: supply the actual passage from *Superintelligence*.

grader_components.part: assumption: technology gives a decisive advantage drives the race
assumption: states put survival first
these assumptions make great-power conflict more likely
a power expecting to lose may strike first rather than accept a rival's permanent dominance
the race is better understood as a trust dilemma than a prisoner's dilemma ('on their account')
powers can in principle cooperate to control the race, and are better off doing so
H/S/W combine deterrence (MAIM: bid for unilateral dominance invites pre-emptive sabotage) with nonproliferation and competitiveness
RAND examines measures intended to stabilise the competition

grader_missing_subclaim: the race is better understood as a trust dilemma than a prisoner's dilemma (attributed via 'on their account')
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Drop the trust-dilemma/prisoner's-dilemma framing (or cite its actual source) and keep only what the cited sources support: because the race is driven by fear rather than pure competition, the powers can in principle cooperate to control it, which RAND argues they are better off doing.
grader_proofs_verified: Destabilizing AI developments could rupture the balance of power and raise the odds of great-power conflict, while widespread proliferation of capable AI hackers and virologists would lower barriers for rogue actors to cause catastrophe.

It seems hard to believe that a great power driven by either ambition for global dominance or fear of being the victim of someone else's decisive technological advantage would allow its AI development to be hamstrung by such a limited threat.

Rather than only relying on cooperation or seeking to outpace their adversaries, they may consider sabotage or datacenter attacks, if the alternative is to accept a future in which one’s national survival is perpetually at risk.

Rather than wait for a rival to weaponize a superintelligence against them, states will act to disable threatening AI projects, producing a deterrence dynamic that might be called Mutual Assured AI Malfunction, or MAIM.
Second, a capability to disable an adversary's pursuit of advanced AI would exacerbate rather than dampen the instability of an AI race by creating potent first-strike incentives.

We introduce the concept of Mutual Assured AI Malfunction (MAIM): adeterrence regime resembling nuclear mutual assured destruction (MAD) where any state’s aggressive bid for unilateral AI dominance is met with preventive sabotage by rivals.
Taken together, the three-part framework of deterrence, nonproliferation, and competitiveness outlines a robust strategy to superintelligence in the years ahead.

In line with the recommendations of Hendrycks, Schmidt, and Wang, the United States could propose collaboration among AI leaders (which would necessarily include private industry) on controlling proliferation in the common interest.
One thing that makes abundantly clear is the need for a strategic concept for managing stability among great powers on the road to transformationally powerful AI.
grader_why: Every factual component and the deterrence/nonproliferation/competitiveness, MAIM, conflict-likelihood, first-strike, and cooperation components are provable from the two cited sources (and mostly already shown). But the interpretive claim that the race is 'better understood as a trust dilemma than a prisoner's dilemma' appears nowhere in either Hendrycks/Schmidt/Wang or RAND — neither phrase occurs in the text. Since at least one component is not provable from the cited sources at all, the author must add a citation for that framing or rewrite it away.
fable_reasoning: MAIM / first-strike incentive proven verbatim. The 'trust dilemma vs prisoner's dilemma' framing and the RAND stabilisation point are uncovered — correctly amber.
